structural_units: AlO6 octahedra sharing faces and edges in hexagonal close-packed oxygen framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Ruby is corundum (Al2O3) colored by Cr3+ substituting for Al3+ in the octahedral site. Chromium content in gem ruby typically 0.1–3 wt% Cr2O3. Iron impurities alongside chromium produce orangey hues; high Cr with low Fe gives the purest reds.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Ruby grade requires both red color from Cr3+ and sufficient saturation. The boundary between 'ruby' and 'pink sapphire' is a commercial convention — there is no discrete compositional boundary, only increasing Cr concentration produces deeper red/pink. 'Pigeon's blood' refers to specific Burmese ruby with vivid red + slight blue/purple fluorescence.

crystallography.twinning: Contact and penetration twins on {0001} and {10-11} common.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Typically prismatic, tabular (hexagonal outline), or barrel-shaped crystals. Alluvial rubies are typically rounded, frosted pebbles. Striated basal faces common. Parting on {0001} and {10-11}.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Second hardest natural mineral (Mohs 9). Extremely durable and abrasion-resistant. No true cleavage but parting planes can affect cutting. High refractive index (~1.76–1.77) gives excellent brilliance.

color_profiles.name: Pigeon's Blood Ruby
colors: pure-red with slight blue fluorescent overtone
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ at optimal concentration (~0.5–1.5% Cr2O3); strong red fluorescence under UV complements natural redness in daylight.
color_profiles.conditions: Mogok Valley, Myanmar — classic marble-hosted ruby environment.

color_profiles.name: Burmese Ruby (general)
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ chromophore; trace Fe adds depth.

color_profiles.name: Thai/Cambodian Ruby
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe content alongside Cr3+ creates darker, less fluorescent red.

color_profiles.name: Star Ruby
colors: red body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Silk inclusions (rutile needles oriented along three crystallographic directions) producing asterism when cut as cabochon.

macro_patterns: fingerprint and needled inclusions under magnification
appearance.visual_notes: Ruby is the red variety of corundum — the intensity of Cr3+ fluorescence in daylight gives Burmese rubies an inner glow that red garnets and spinel cannot replicate. In strong sunlight, a fine Burmese ruby appears to emit light. The six-rayed star in star ruby is one of the most captivating optical effects in gemology.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Strong red fluorescence under UV (LW and SW) — Cr3+ emission; Burmese/marble-hosted rubies fluoresce more strongly than basalt-hosted Thai rubies (which have higher Fe quenching fluorescence)
optical_and_special_effects.phosphorescence: Some Burmese rubies phosphoresce after UV exposure
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star ruby — from intersecting rutile (silk) needle inclusions parallel to three directions in {0001} basal plane
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Dichroic: orange-red / purple-red (strong); orientation critical in faceting for color optimization
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. nω ≈ 1.770, nε ≈ 1.762 (RI ~1.762–1.770). Birefringence ~0.008. Strong fluorescence under UV is diagnostic for distinguishing ruby from red spinel, garnet.

notable_localities: Mogok Valley, Sagaing Region, Myanmar (world's finest — marble-hosted pigeon's blood)
Mong Hsu, Shan State, Myanmar (commercial production; requires heat treatment)
Vietnam (Lục Yên and Quỳ Châu — marble-hosted)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Marble-hosted rubies (Myanmar, Vietnam) tend to have low Fe and strong fluorescence — producing the coveted 'pigeon's blood' appearance. Basalt-hosted rubies (Thailand, Cambodia) have higher Fe that quenches fluorescence and deepens/darkens color. Mozambique has become a leading producer since major deposits discovered at Montepuez.

historical_uses: Gemstone in jewelry — highest value among colored stones historically
Set in royal regalia across Asian and European monarchies
Ruby laser (first working laser, Theodore Maiman, 1960 — used synthetic ruby)
Watch bearings and precision instrument components (synthetic ruby)
cultural_associations: Burmese warriors believed rubies made them invulnerable in battle — inserted under skin
Hindu texts: ruby at the navel of Vishnu's crown
European medieval belief: ruby darkens when its owner is in danger
Ruby associated with Mars (Roman), divine protection, and the blood of the gods
The Black Prince's Ruby in the British Imperial State Crown — actually red spinel, not ruby
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Ruby is the 'king of gems' — a title given in Sanskrit texts that has persisted across cultures. Burmese warriors inserted rubies under their skin before battle believing it would make them invincible. Medieval European tradition held that a ruby's color deepened to warn its wearer of approaching danger and paled if the wearer was in mortal peril. The famous 'Black Prince's Ruby' in the British Crown Jewels is actually a large red spinel — a misidentification that held for centuries. The first ruby laser (1960) used chromium-doped synthetic corundum, the same physics as natural ruby's red fluorescence.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Ruby holds the deep-red that human eyes are tuned to desire — the color of blood, roses, and fire condensed into Mohs-9 corundum. In strong daylight, Burmese pigeon's blood ruby appears to glow from within — the chromium fluorescence adding spectral energy that no mere pigment can produce.

practical_notes.handling_notes: Extremely durable in gem form. Second hardest natural material. Avoid impacts on girdle (parting planes can cleave). Safe for all normal jewelry use.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water and brush; ultrasonic generally safe (avoid for heavily fractured or filled stones). Store separately from other gems to avoid mutual scratching.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: universal and accepted by the trade; removes silk, improves color and clarity
Fracture filling with lead glass: used for heavily included material; requires disclosure and special care
Beryllium diffusion: produces color alteration; requires disclosure
Flux-grown synthetic ruby: Chatham, Ramaura — identified by flux inclusions under magnification
Verneuil (flame-fusion) synthetic ruby: curved growth lines, gas bubbles
Hydrothermal synthetic ruby: very close to natural; spectroscopic testing required
common_confusions: Red spinel (isometric — no birefringence; no fluorescence at same intensity; The 'Black Prince's Ruby' in British Crown is spinel)
Pyrope garnet (no birefringence; no UV fluorescence)
Synthetic ruby (requires spectroscopic and inclusion analysis)
practical_notes.market_notes: Ruby is the highest-priced colored gemstone per carat globally. Burmese (Mogok) pigeon's blood rubies with no heat treatment command extreme premiums at auction. Mozambique rubies have emerged as a credible high-value alternative. Treatment disclosure is mandatory in the trade. Synthetic rubies are commodity items in industry.

prompt_fragments: pigeon's blood ruby — chromium fire glowing in Mogok marble
star ruby cabochon with six-rayed silk asterism
vivid red fluorescent corundum — blood of gems

provenance.notes: Ruby is a variety of corundum. mindat_id 3486 is the GIA/mindat entry for ruby variety specifically.
